title: Spark-Gap Tesla Coil from Household Power
summary: Two high school students designed and built a fully functional spark-gap Tesla coil powered by a standard 220V household outlet, achieving complete wireless LED illumination and visible arc discharges. The final system produced 4 cm sparks using a two-storey MDF frame, oil-cooled transformer, and custom safety control panel.
details:
This upper-secondary practical project was motivated by a sustained interest in electrophysics and high-voltage demonstrations. Working collaboratively to share costs and combine complementary skills, the two students set out to build a resonant transformer capable of producing visible electrical arcs using only standard household power—no industrial three-phase supply. The project included full documentation in both Estonian and English, a formal safety manual under the brand name KR Engineering, and a comprehensive academic report submitted to Miina Härma Gymnasium in January 2025.

The Tesla coil operates by stepping 220V AC mains up to approximately 4 kV using a salvaged microwave oven transformer submerged in mineral oil for arc suppression. This charges a bank of three parallel capacitors until the voltage exceeds the breakdown threshold of a custom spark gap, at which point the energy discharges in high-frequency bursts through a flat spiral primary coil. The rapidly changing magnetic field induces far higher voltage in a tightly wound secondary coil with many more turns, creating an electric field at the toroidal terminal strong enough to ionize air and produce visible discharges. The high-frequency, high-impedance nature of the output makes direct contact survivable for ungrounded individuals without medical implants.

Construction took place primarily in August 2024 and again intensively in October–November 2024 after the first prototype revealed critical design flaws. The initial build suffered a transformer failure due to internal short circuits, prompting the switch to an oil-bath enclosure. More significantly, soldered connections were replaced throughout with copper crimp connectors, dramatically reducing contact resistance and improving arc performance. The final design uses a two-storey MDF frame: the lower floor houses the capacitor bank, spark gap, and oil-cooled transformer, while the upper floor contains the primary coil, secondary coil, and toroid. A grounding conductor runs through both levels.

The completed coil successfully demonstrated 4 cm arc discharges to grounded metal objects and wirelessly illuminated an LED bulb held near the secondary coil via electromagnetic induction. Performance was evaluated against four weighted criteria: arc length/power (4/5), safety (4/5), practicality (5/5), and visual finish (4/5), yielding an overall score of 4/5. Both students noted that earlier initiation of the physical build would have allowed time for second-hand component sourcing and reduced the €82 budget overrun. The project provided hands-on experience with resonance, impedance matching, and high-frequency phenomena typically encountered only in first- or second-year university electrical engineering courses.
